skill.name: python-style-guide
skill.description: Python style guidance for writing, reviewing, and refactoring code using Google-style conventions, type annotations, docstrings, imports, naming, and common anti-patterns.
skill.summary: Apply practical Python style rules for cleaner, more maintainable code.
skill.author: CodingKaiser

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation false positives. The skill contains Markdown guidance, safe and unsafe Python examples, license text, and example URLs; no executable scripts, command runners, secret access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection attempts were found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown backticks and Python examples in documentation. These files do not define executable shell commands or tool scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown examples or inline code in reference material. I found no execution wrapper, shell script, or instruction that asks the agent to run these snippets.

low_findings.title: False Positive: eval Example Is Labeled Unsafe
low_findings.description: The eval and exec detections occur in an anti-pattern section. The example is explicitly marked wrong and followed by a safer ast.literal_eval alternative.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context confirms this is defensive guidance, not an instruction to execute dynamic code. The same section labels eval as a major security risk.

low_findings.title: False Positive: License Prose Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and reconnaissance hits in the license are Creative Commons legal text. They are not cryptographic code and do not perform system inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss copyright, attribution, technical modifications, and warranty disclaimers. There is no algorithm implementation, import, command, or runtime behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Example URLs Are Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The detected URLs are a Google style guide citation and an api.example.com docstring example. They do not transmit data or collect credentials.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The first URL is a source citation, and the second appears inside an illustrative docstring example. I found no evidence of automatic network access or exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Python teams need consistent rules for readable, maintainable code. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused Python style reference for writing, reviewing, and refactoring.
actual_capabilities: Applies Python import, naming, formatting, and blank-line conventions.
Reviews code for common Python anti-patterns and maintainability issues.
Guides Google-style docstrings for modules, functions, classes, and tests.
Recommends modern type annotation patterns for Python 3.9 and newer.
Explains safer alternatives to risky patterns such as broad exceptions and eval.
Helps refactor Python examples toward clearer, idiomatic structure.
limitations: It is a written reference, not an automated linter or formatter.
It does not execute tests or prove runtime behavior.
It may need adaptation for projects using different local conventions.
It does not replace security review for production Python services.

use_cases.title: Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check Python changes for style, naming, imports, docstrings, and avoidable anti-patterns before merge.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Modules
use_cases.description: Convert inconsistent Python code toward clearer annotations, safer defaults, and more readable control flow.

use_cases.title: Teach Team Conventions
use_cases.description: Give junior developers practical examples of Google-style Python conventions and common mistakes to avoid.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Style
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small function or module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python code for naming, imports, formatting, and simple readability issues. Explain each recommended change briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Docstrings
prompt_templates.scenario: Add or revise documentation for public APIs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite the docstrings in this Python code using Google-style sections. Keep the behavior unchanged and note missing parameter details.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor With Types
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Python module using modern type annotations, safer defaults, and clearer structure. Preserve behavior and explain tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Anti-Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Python change for style-guide violations, anti-patterns, risky exception handling, mutable defaults, and unclear abstractions. Prioritize findings by impact.

output_examples.input: A function has a mutable default list, broad exception handling, and no return annotation.
output_examples.output: The review recommends using None as the default, catching a specific exception, adding a return type, and documenting the behavior.

output_examples.input: A public class has sparse comments and inconsistent method names.
output_examples.output: The response proposes Google-style class and method docstrings, clearer lower_with_under method names, and focused comments only where needed.

output_examples.input: A module mixes standard library imports with project imports and has complex list comprehensions.
output_examples.output: The guidance separates import groups, alphabetizes them, and replaces the dense comprehension with a readable loop.

best_practices: Ask for a prioritized review when applying the guide to a large diff.
Tell the assistant which project conventions override the default guide.
Request behavior-preserving refactors when changing existing code.
anti_patterns: Do not ask the assistant to apply style rules without showing the relevant code.
Do not accept formatting changes that conflict with the project linter.
Do not treat style guidance as a substitute for tests or security review.

faq.question: Does this skill run Python code?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation-based style guide for AI-assisted writing, review, and refactoring.

faq.question: Which style guide does it follow?
faq.answer: It is based on Google Python style conventions and includes local practical examples.

faq.question: Can it replace Black, Ruff, or mypy?
faq.answer: No. It complements those tools by explaining review decisions and style intent.

faq.question: Can it help with type annotations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for modern built-in generics, unions, protocols, type aliases, and TypeVars.

faq.question: Can it write docstrings?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides Google-style docstring examples for functions, classes, exceptions, async code, and tests.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples and license text, not executable malware behavior.
